section.title: Israel's Unbelief
section.sectionRationale: Having said that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, Paul traces the chain backward: to call, one must believe; to believe, one must hear; to hear, someone must preach; to preach, one must be sent. So faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ. The gospel has indeed gone out, yet not all obeyed. Paul closes the chapter by pressing home Israel's guilt: they heard and understood, yet remained disobedient and obstinate, while God provoked them to jealousy by a people who were not a nation and held out His hands all day long to a resistant people.
section.thesis: Salvation runs through the sent and preached word, for faith comes from hearing and hearing through the word of Christ; Israel's failure is therefore not for want of hearing or understanding but is the culpable unbelief of a disobedient and obstinate people, toward whom God has patiently held out His hands all day long.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the chain of salvation and the necessity of the preached word, so that faith comes by hearing the word of Christ; and Israel's inexcusable unbelief, for they heard and understood, yet remained disobedient while God stretched out His hands to them all day long.

units.label: Faith comes by hearing the word
units.note: the chain of calling, believing, hearing, preaching, sending

verses.text: How then can they call on the One in whom they have not believed? And how can they believe in the One of whom they have not heard? And how can they hear without someone to {{kerysso|preach}}?
And how can they preach unless they are sent? As it is written: “How beautiful are the feet of those who bring {{euangelizo|good news}}!”
But not all of them welcomed the good news. For Isaiah says, “Lord, who has believed our message?”
Consequently, {{pistis|faith}} comes by {{akoe|hearing}}, and hearing by the {{rhema|word}} of Christ.

units.label: Israel's inexcusable unbelief
units.note: they heard and understood, yet remained obstinate

verses.text: But I ask, did they not hear? Indeed they did: “Their voice has gone out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world.”
I ask instead, did Israel not understand? First, Moses says: “I will make you jealous by those who are not a nation; I will make you angry by a nation without understanding.”
And Isaiah boldly says: “I was found by those who did not seek Me; I revealed Myself to those who did not ask for Me.”
But as for Israel he says: “All day long I have held out My hands to a disobedient and obstinate people.”

kerysso.gloss: To proclaim as a herald, to preach. A herald does not invent his message but announces the word of his king. Paul insists that people cannot hear the gospel without a preacher, so the public heralding of Christ is an indispensable link in the chain that leads sinners to call on the Lord and be saved.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to preach the gospel. Isaiah celebrated the beautiful feet of those who bring good news of peace; Paul applies it to the messengers of Christ. The word ties the whole chain to the gospel itself, the glad announcement of salvation accomplished in the crucified and risen Lord.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Paul states its origin plainly: faith comes from hearing. It is not self-generated but awakened by the message of Christ, the empty hand that receives the salvation announced in the gospel. Where the word of Christ is heard, faith is born.

akoe.gloss: Hearing, or the thing heard, the report or message. Paul says faith comes by akoe, the hearing of the message, and that message is the word of Christ. The term links the act of hearing and the content heard, underscoring that faith is bound to the gospel that reaches the ear.

rhema.gloss: A spoken word, an utterance. The hearing that produces faith comes by the rhema of Christ, the spoken message about and from Christ. It is not vague religious feeling but the definite, proclaimed word of Christ that carries God's saving power to the hearer.

basic.context: Paul has just declared that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved. Now he asks how people come to call at all, and traces the chain backward: calling depends on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching, preaching on being sent. This is why the gospel must be proclaimed. Then Paul confronts Israel's unbelief directly: the message went out and they heard and understood it, yet they refused, while God kept holding out His hands to them.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chain in verses 14 and 15 forward and backward: God sends preachers, who preach, so that people hear, believe, and call. Verse 17 is the hinge: faith comes by hearing the word of Christ. Then verses 18 to 21 remove every excuse from Israel. They did hear; they did understand; their problem was not ignorance but a hardened, disobedient heart, which makes their unbelief culpable and God's patience all the more striking.
basic.originalLanguages: The chain requires a herald who will kerysso, preach, and messengers who euangelizo, bring good news. Its result is pistis, faith, which comes by akoe, hearing, and hearing by the rhema, the spoken word, of Christ. The whole passage binds saving faith to the proclaimed gospel.
basic.typology: Isaiah's beautiful feet of those who bring good news, first spoken of heralds announcing return from exile, are fulfilled in the messengers of Christ who announce the greater deliverance. The provoking of Israel to jealousy by a people who are not a nation points forward to the ingathering of the Gentiles.
basic.christ: Faith comes by the word of Christ; He is the content of the message that saves. He is the good news the heralds bring, the Lord on whom people call. The one whom Israel refused is the one God has been holding out His hands to offer them, so that their unbelief is a rejection of Christ Himself.
basic.god: God is the sender who commissions preachers and the one whose word goes out to the ends of the earth. He is found by those who did not seek Him, revealing His grace to the Gentiles, and He is the patient God who all day long holds out His hands to a disobedient people. His sovereignty in sending and His grief over unbelief stand side by side.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 52 speaks of the beautiful feet of the messenger. Isaiah 53 asks who has believed our message. Psalm 19 says their voice has gone out to all the earth. Deuteronomy 32 and Isaiah 65 speak of provoking Israel to jealousy and God's outstretched hands.
basic.oneStory: God's plan to save everyone who calls on the Lord moves forward through the sent and preached word, so that faith is born by hearing the gospel of Christ. Yet the same chapter shows the sorrow at the heart of the story: Israel heard and understood, yet largely refused, while God stretched out His hands to them and began drawing in the nations who never sought Him.

context.literary: These verses complete Romans 10, flowing directly from the promise that everyone who calls on the Lord will be saved. Verses 14 to 17 form a tightly linked chain of rhetorical questions, and verses 18 to 21 answer two objections, did they not hear, did they not understand, each settled by Scripture. The chapter ends on the poignant image of God's outstretched hands, setting up chapter eleven.
context.historical: Paul writes as the apostle to the Gentiles who has seen the gospel spread across the empire while many of his own people reject it. The chain of sending and preaching reflects the missionary reality of the early church, and Israel's refusal, despite full exposure to the message, is the painful backdrop against which Paul will unfold God's ongoing purpose for Israel.
context.canonical: Romans 10:14-17 became a foundational text for the church's theology of preaching and mission, and verse 17, faith comes by hearing, a watchword of the Reformation's stress on the word. The closing citations weave together the Law, the Psalms, and the Prophets to show that Israel's unbelief and the Gentiles' inclusion were foretold in Israel's own Scriptures.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that salvation depends on the sent and preached word, so that the church must proclaim the gospel, and to establish that Israel's failure is not due to any lack of hearing or understanding but is culpable unbelief, thereby upholding both God's justice and His patient, outstretched grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chain of sending, preaching, hearing, believing, and calling both describes how the gospel actually spreads and prescribes the necessity of proclamation for every age. The account of Israel's unbelief describes a historical reality but carries the abiding warning that hearing without faith leaves one inexcusable before God.

debates.question: Does faith comes by hearing make the outward preaching of the word necessary for salvation?

views.label: The ordinary and necessary means
views.case: The historic reading holds that God has appointed the preached word as the ordinary means by which faith is awakened; hence the chain of sending and preaching, and the plain statement that faith comes by hearing the word of Christ. The church is therefore bound to send and to preach, for this is God's normal way of saving.

views.label: An absolute rule without exception
views.case: Some press the verse to mean no one is ever saved apart from hearing a human preacher; this overreaches, since Scripture also shows God sovereignly working, yet it rightly honors the priority Paul gives to the proclaimed word.

debates.bearing: The passage foregrounds the necessity and dignity of gospel proclamation as God's appointed means, summoning the church to send and preach, while leaving God free in His sovereignty; the settled point is that faith is bound to the word of Christ and does not arise from within.

debates.question: Is Israel's unbelief excusable on the grounds that they did not hear or understand?

views.label: Inexcusable, culpable unbelief
views.case: Paul answers his own questions with Scripture: they did hear, for the message went out to the ends of the earth, and they did understand, as Moses and Isaiah foretold. Their unbelief is therefore willful, the response of a disobedient and obstinate people, and cannot be excused by ignorance.

views.label: Mitigated by hardening
views.case: Some point to God's sovereign hardening in chapter nine and elsewhere to soften Israel's guilt; yet Paul here places the accent squarely on their disobedience and obstinacy, holding human responsibility firmly even alongside God's sovereignty.

debates.bearing: The passage upholds both truths without collapsing either: God is sovereign, yet Israel's unbelief is genuinely their own fault, for they heard and understood and still refused the God who all day long held out His hands to them, so that His justice and His mercy are both magnified.

study.originalLanguages: The chain of salvation requires a herald to kerysso, to preach, and messengers who euangelizo, who bring the good news whose feet Isaiah calls beautiful. Its fruit is pistis, faith, and Paul states its origin exactly: faith comes by akoe, hearing, and that hearing comes by the rhema, the spoken word, of Christ. The vocabulary binds saving faith to the definite, proclaimed message about Christ, not to any inner impulse, so that the sending and preaching of the gospel are shown to be indispensable to the salvation of everyone who calls on the Lord.

typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's herald announcing good news of peace and return is fulfilled in the messengers of Christ who proclaim the greater deliverance of the gospel to all nations.

typology.type: A people who are not a nation
typology.fulfillment: Moses foretold that God would provoke Israel to jealousy by those who are not a nation, fulfilled as the Gentiles are gathered in and Israel is stirred.

typology.type: Found by those who did not seek
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's word about God revealing Himself to those who did not ask is fulfilled in the calling of the Gentiles, who found the God they never sought.

study.christ: Christ is the substance of the whole passage even where He is not named in every line. Faith comes by the word of Christ, so He is the content of the saving message; He is the good news the heralds carry and the Lord on whom sinners call. The message Isaiah lamented as unbelieved, who has believed our report, is ultimately the report of Christ crucified and risen. And the God who all day long stretches out His hands to a disobedient people does so to offer them His Son. To refuse the message is to refuse Christ Himself.
study.god: God is the sovereign sender who commissions and sends preachers, and the one whose word has gone out to the ends of the earth. He is the God found by those who did not seek Him, freely revealing His grace to the Gentiles who never asked. And He is the wounded, patient God who all day long holds out His hands to a disobedient and obstinate people. His sovereignty in the spread of the gospel and His genuine, grieving appeal to sinners stand together, magnifying both His power and His mercy.

crossReferences.note: How beautiful are the feet of the messenger who brings good news, applied to the heralds of Christ.
Lord, who has believed our message, showing that unbelief toward the gospel was foretold.
Their voice has gone out into all the earth, cited to show the message truly went forth.
God found by those who did not seek Him, and His hands held out all day long to a disobedient people.

study.oneStory: God's saving purpose advances through the sent and preached word, so that faith is born as people hear the gospel of Christ, and the church is commissioned to carry it to the ends of the earth. Yet the same chapter voices the ache running through Scripture: Israel heard the message and understood it, yet largely refused, while God revealed Himself to the nations who never sought Him and stretched out His hands all day long to His own obstinate people. The story now waits for chapter eleven and the question of whether God is finished with Israel.

quiz.q: What is the chain Paul traces in verses 14 and 15?

options.text: Calling depends on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching, and preaching on being sent.
options.note: Paul links calling, believing, hearing, preaching, and sending in a connected chain.

options.text: Salvation depends only on an inner feeling with no outward message.
options.note: The whole point is that a preacher must be sent and the word must be heard.

options.text: People can believe without ever hearing the message.
options.note: Paul asks how they can believe in One of whom they have not heard; hearing is necessary.

options.text: Preachers may preach without being sent.
options.note: Paul asks how they can preach unless they are sent; sending is part of the chain.

quiz.q: According to verse 17, where does faith come from?

options.text: Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of Christ.
options.note: Verse 17 grounds faith in the hearing of the proclaimed word of Christ.

options.text: Faith arises from within by human willpower.
options.note: Paul roots faith in hearing the word, not in self-generated resolve.

options.text: Faith comes chiefly by keeping the law.
options.note: Faith comes by hearing the word of Christ, not by law keeping.

options.text: Faith comes mainly by miracles apart from any message.
options.note: Paul names the word of Christ, the heard message, as the source of faith.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer the objection that Israel did not hear?

options.text: They did hear; their voice has gone out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world.
options.note: Verse 18 quotes Psalm 19 to show the message truly reached them.

options.text: He admits they never had a real chance to hear.
options.note: Paul insists they did hear; the message went out to all.

options.text: He says only a few of them heard it.
options.note: Paul says the message went out widely, not merely to a few.

options.text: He leaves the question unanswered.
options.note: Paul answers it directly with Scripture: indeed they did hear.

options.text: Because they both heard and understood the message, yet remained a disobedient and obstinate people.
options.note: Paul settles both objections: they heard, verse 18, and understood, verse 19, yet refused.

options.text: Because they were never given the law or the promises.
options.note: Israel had every privilege, as chapter nine listed; the fault was unbelief.

options.text: Because the message was too obscure to grasp.
options.note: Paul shows they did understand; the message was not obscure.

options.text: Because God never appealed to them.
options.note: God held out His hands to them all day long; the appeal was constant.

quiz.q: How does the chapter portray God's posture toward Israel in verse 21?

options.text: As one who all day long holds out His hands to a disobedient and obstinate people.
options.note: Verse 21 pictures God's patient, grieving appeal to His resistant people.

options.text: As one who has already destroyed them in anger.
options.note: God is shown appealing with outstretched hands, not destroying them.

options.text: As one who is indifferent to their response.
options.note: Far from indifferent, God stretches out His hands all day long.

options.text: As one who never offered Himself to them at all.
options.note: God repeatedly held out His hands; the offer was continual.

questions: Faith comes by hearing the word of Christ. How does this shape the value you place on hearing and sharing the gospel, and on sending others to preach it?
Paul says how beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news. Whom might God be calling you to carry the good news to, near or far?
Israel heard and understood yet refused. Are there truths of the gospel you clearly grasp but have been slow to obey from the heart?
God was found by those who did not seek Him. How does it move you that God took the initiative to reveal Himself to people, perhaps including you, who were not even looking for Him?
God all day long held out His hands to a disobedient people. How does this picture of God's patient appeal change the way you think about His heart toward sinners, and your own hardness?

translationNotes.note: Those who bring good news renders the messengers whose feet Isaiah 52:7 calls beautiful. A textual footnote notes a longer form in some manuscripts, who bring good news of peace, who bring good news of good things; the sense is unchanged.

The word of Christ renders rhema Christou, which can mean the word about Christ or the word spoken by Christ through His heralds; both senses point to the proclaimed gospel message as the source of faith.

Disobedient and obstinate renders two terms for a people who both refuse to obey and stubbornly contradict; the image of God's hands held out all day long, from Isaiah 65:2, conveys patient, persistent appeal.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 10:14-21. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
